statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Zhily and Jily resolved to travel across the entire world to eliminate all chaos. They start from a certain place and wish to visit every region exactly once.

You are given a directed graph with $ n $ vertices numbered from $ 1 $ to $ n $ . For each vertex $ i $ ( $ 1 \le i \le n $ ), there are directed edges from $ i $ to all vertices $ j $ such that $ a_i \le j \le n $ .

Find a Hamiltonian cycle $ ^{\text{∗}} $ of the graph.

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ A Hamiltonian cycle is a cycle that visits each vertex exactly once.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n\,(1 \leq n \leq 10^5) $ — the number of vertices.

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ a_1,a_2,\cdots,a_n\,(1 \leq a_i \leq n) $ describing the graph.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 10^6 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, if no Hamiltonian cycle exists, output "No" in one line.

Otherwise, output "Yes" on the first line. On the second line, output a permutation $ p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n $ ( $ 1\le p_i\le n $ ) representing the order of vertices visited in the cycle. If there are multiple solutions, print any of them.

You can output the answer in any case (upper or lower). For example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes", and "YES" will be recognized as positive responses.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
7
1 3 6 3 2 1 5
9
4 2 9 8 5 8 5 7 9
8
3 4 4 5 6 1 8 2
1
1
5
5 4 2 5 5
Output
Yes
7 5 2 4 3 6 1
No
Yes
1 3 7 8 2 4 5 6
Yes
1
No

Note
In the first test case, the figure below illustrates a Hamiltonian cycle: $ 1 \to 7 \to 5 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 6 \to 1 $ .

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2223D/e795c804cc2b0544915d31bdaed1afe59bf01100df8caf699f79a03002f5def2.png)In the second test case, no Hamiltonian cycle exists because there are no incoming edges to vertex $ 1 $ (i.e., no vertex can reach vertex $ 1 $ ).

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2223D - Zhily and Cycle
Assume there exists a Hamiltonian cycle, where point $$$i$$$ connects to $$$u_i$$$. Then, the edge $$$(i, u_i)$$$ can be split into two edges: $$$(i, a_i)$$$ and $$$(a_i, u_i)$$$, noting that $$$a_i \le u_i$$$ always holds.
Consider constructing a graph $$$G = (V, E)$$$, where $$$V = \{1, 2, \cdots, n\}$$$ and $$$E = \{(i, a_i) | 1 \le i \le n\}$$$. And if there exists a Hamiltonian cycle, there should exist an Eulerian circuit after adding some edges of the form $$$(a_i, u_i)$$$ to this graph. We only need to determine whether it is possible to add $$$n$$$ edges of the form $$$(a_i, u_i)$$$ to make $$$G$$$ an Eulerian graph.
Note that relaxing the restriction on adding edges does not affect the determination result. We can allow the addition of any number of edges $$$(u, v)$$$ satisfying $$$u  \lt  v$$$. Under such conditions, whether $$$G$$$ can become an Eulerian graph is equivalent to whether $$$G$$$ can become an Eulerian graph under the original conditions.
Consider how to determine whether there exists a scheme for adding edges. The conditions for an Eulerian graph are that the out-degree and in-degree of each vertex are equal and the graph is connected. Let the out-degree and in-degree of vertex $$$i$$$ be denoted as $$$out_i$$$ and $$$in_i$$$, respectively, and define $$$deg_i = out_i - in_i$$$. Consider the degree sequence of $$$G$$$: $$$deg_1, deg_2, \cdots, deg_n$$$. Each time, we can choose $$$i  \lt  j$$$ and update $$$deg_i \leftarrow deg_i + 1$$$ and $$$deg_j \leftarrow deg_j - 1$$$. Ultimately, we need to make all $$$deg_i$$$ equal to $$$0$$$ while ensuring the graph remains connected.
Let $$$sum_i = \sum_{u = 1}^{n} deg_u$$$. It is important to note that the condition for all $$$deg_i$$$ to be $$$0$$$ can be satisfied if and only if $$$\forall 1 \le i \le n, sum_i \le 0$$$. Therefore, we can add edges arbitrarily under this condition and see if we can make the graph connected.
Find all indices where $$$sum_i = 0$$$, denoting them as $$$t_0 = 0  \lt  1 \le t_1  \lt  t_2  \lt  \cdots  \lt  t_m = n$$$ (it is clear that $$$sum_n = 0$$$). Any edge we add cannot cross over $$$t_i$$$. Otherwise, if there exists $$$u \le t_i  \lt  v$$$ and we add $$$(u, v)$$$, then it must be the case that $$$sum_{t_i}  \gt  0$$$, making it impossible to satisfy the degree constraints with any subsequent edge additions.
On the other hand, we can add all edges of the form $$$(i, i + 1)$$$ where $$$i \neq t_j$$$. If the graph is still not connected at this point, then it will never be possible to connect it regardless of how edges are added. Thus, we first add all such edges, and then greedily pair the remaining vertices with unbalanced in-degrees and out-degrees, adding no more than $$$3n$$$ edges in total.
If the above process is completed successfully, we will have obtained an Eulerian graph. We only need to find any Eulerian circuit in it to reconstruct a Hamiltonian cycle in the original graph.
The total complexity is $$$O(n)$$$
Also, there's an another solution given by gemini as follows.
We first sort $$$1, \cdots, n$$$ according to $$$a_i$$$ in increasing order, and denote the result sequence by $$$u_1, \cdots, u_n$$$, satisfying $$$a_{u_i}  \lt  a_{u_{i + 1}}$$$ for all $$$1\le i  \lt  n$$$. Then we simply link all the edges $$$(u_i, i)$$$. If the result doesn't meet the constraint $$$i \ge a_{u_i}$$$, it can be easily proved that the constraint can never be met and thus there isn't a Hamiltonian cycle in the graph.
And if the constraint is met, all left for us to do is to adjust the graph into one component. We check all pairs $$$(i, i + 1)$$$. If $$$i$$$ and $$$i + 1$$$ are not in the same component and $$$a_{u_{i + 1}} \le i$$$ (which means the following operation won't obey the constraint given by $$$a_i$$$), we alter the end points of $$$u_i$$$ and $$$u_{i + 1}$$$. Now let's prove that we can always find a Hamiltonian cycle after these operations if there is one in the graph.
Denote the node pointing to $$$i$$$ in the graph after the operations by $$$v_i$$$, and then we get the sequence $$$v_i$$$. If the graph still isn't connected, we denote the component containing $$$1$$$ with $$$S$$$ and all the other nodes as $$$T$$$($$$T = \{1, \cdots, n\} - S$$$), and they will form several consecutive segments on both the sequence $$$1, \cdots, n$$$ and $$$v_1, \cdots, v_n$$$(the indexes are corresponding). Since all valid operations have been done, the beginning of every segment will always satisfy that $$$a_{v_i} = i$$$. We claim that under these conditions there can never be a Hamiltonian cycle in the graph.
Consider how could we adjust the result to build a Hamiltonian cycle. To do this, we must build an edge pointing from $$$T$$$ to $$$S$$$, and let's assume it's $$$(v_i, j)$$$, where $$$v_i\in T, j\in S, i  \lt  j$$$. Consider which node will $$$v_j$$$ point to. It will be in either the segment containing $$$j$$$ or the following segments. If is the former, trace the augment path and the destination will always be in the following segments, which is the latter case. Thus, we will always have to build the edge $$$(v_j, k)$$$ where $$$v_j\in S, k\in T, j  \lt  k$$$. Doing the operation repeatedly and we will find conflict in the last segment.
So in summary, we can always find the Hamiltonian cycle if there is one. The whole algorithm can be implemented by DSU with time complexity $$$O(n\log n)$$$ bounding on the sort.
